title: Cat Dragging Hind Legs: Emergency Triage and Causes
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its hind legs or appears paralyzed, it is a life-threatening emergency. Learn about FATE and why you need a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind leg paralysis in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency usually caused by a blood clot, requiring immediate 24-hour veterinary intervention.

What causes sudden hind leg paralysis in cats?

Sudden hind leg paralysis, often characterized by a cat dragging its back legs and crying out in distress, is usually a sign of a severe underlying medical crisis. The most common cause is Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), also known as a 'Saddle Thrombus.' This occurs when a blood clot, often originating from a diseased heart, travels through the aorta and lodges at the junction where blood flows to the back legs, effectively cutting off circulation.

Is my cat dragging its back legs a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority, life-threatening emergency.
  • A cat dragging its hind legs is experiencing extreme pain and critical oxygen deprivation to its tissues.
  • Without immediate veterinary intervention, this condition is fatal or can lead to permanent limb damage.
  • Do not wait for an appointment; proceed immediately to the nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my cat?

  • While your primary focus should be on getting to the vet, a quick video of how your cat is moving or a photo of their posture is invaluable for the medical team.
  • These visual aids allow the triage nurse to quickly differentiate between neurological issues, orthopedic injuries, or vascular blocks.
  • This information helps the clinical team prepare necessary pain relief and diagnostic equipment before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with hindlimb paresis or paralysis, arterial thromboembolism should be a primary differential. Clinical signs include pain, paresis or paralysis of the hindlimbs, reduced or absent arterial pulses (femoral or pedal) in the affected limbs, which are cooler than normal, and firm, swollen gastrocnemius muscles. Diagnosis is based on clinical signs, physical examination findings, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs; ultrasound can also be used to identify the thromboembolus. Doppler ultrasonography helps evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries. Any condition resulting in left atrial enlargement, including cardiomyopathies, hyperthyroidism, or congenital heart disease, can predispose to arterial thromboembolism. Management consists of analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease. Anticoagulants, such as unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin, are used to reduce continued thrombus formation. Neurologic deficits may improve within 2-3 weeks, but 6 months may be required for complete recovery, and permanent deficits are possible.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 127)

faqs.question: Are my cat's legs cold to the touch?
faqs.answer: If your cat is dragging its legs due to a blood clot, the paws will often feel noticeably cold and the paw pads may appear bluish or pale due to the lack of blood flow.

faqs.question: Can hind leg paralysis be treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. For blood clots, it involves intensive pain management and anticoagulants. For spinal injuries, surgery or rest may be required. Immediate care significantly improves the chances of a positive outcome.

faqs.question: Should I give my cat aspirin or human pain meds?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications. Many are toxic to cats and can complicate the life-saving treatments the veterinarian needs to administer.
